title: 【吉布提】美國國防部正式投訴　【南海】白宮警告將面臨後果　準備採取措施

replies.msg:
http://news.rthk.hk/rthk/ch/component/k2/1394568-20180504.htm

美軍機疑被中方激光照射　白宮警告將面臨後果
2018-05-04 HKT 03:25


桑德斯警告中方將面臨短長期後果。（美聯社資料圖片）

美國白宮發言人桑德斯表示，對中國在南海島礁的軍事化，以及美國軍機懷疑被中國軍方的激光瞄準，美國政府準備採取措施。

她回應提問時說，已經向中方直接提出關注，又警告將會有短期和長期後果。

美國國防部指，美軍戰機在東非國家吉布提附近飛行時，多次被相信是解放軍駐當地基地發出的激光照射，已經正式向中方投訴。

http://news.rthk.hk/rthk/ch/component/k2/1394564-20180504.htm

美國防部投訴中方在吉布提以激光照射美軍戰機
2018-05-04 HKT 02:39


解放軍軍艦開往吉布提基地。（資料圖片）

美國國防部正式向中方投訴，指美軍戰機在東非國家吉布提附近飛行時，多次被相信是中方的激光照射。

五角大樓指，過去數星期已經有近10次類似事件，指激光屬於軍用級別，形容事件嚴重，已經要求中國政府調查。

美國聯邦航空管理局相信，激光是在距離解放軍駐吉布提基地對開海面750米發射。

美方官員透露，上月一架C-130運輸機上兩名機師，被激光照射後眼晴輕微受傷。

管理局自上月14日起，多次發出警告，提醒軍機飛越有關空域時要「極之謹慎」。
解放軍駐吉布提基地去年8月啟用，是中國首個海外基地。

http://www.881903.com/Page/ZH-TW/newsdetail.aspx?ItemId=1006364

美國防部指中方在吉布提使用激光令2美軍眼部受傷

04.05.2018 03:18

美國國防部表示，已正式向中國提出投訴，指中方在解放軍位於非洲吉布提的基地附近，對美軍軍機使用激光，導致兩名美軍飛行員眼部受輕傷。五角大樓發言人表示，飛行員的報告指有三次來自解放軍基地附近的軍事級激光照射活動，認為事件對美國空軍構成嚴重威脅， 美國已經要求中國調查事件。美國國防部並向美軍飛行員發出通知，指在吉布提基地附近飛行時要高度警惕。

解放軍駐吉布提基地在去年8月正式投入使用，是解放軍首個海外基地，距離美軍在吉布提的軍事基地只有約12公里。
呢條桑德斯啲嘴臉仲唔開胃過華嬸

https://www.politico.com/story/2018/05/03/white-house-consequences-south-china-sea-566763


Sarah Huckabee Sanders Sanders said the Trump administration has “raised concerns” about the measures “directly” with Chinese authorities. | Saul Loeb/Getty Images

White House warns of ‘consequences’ for defense build-up in South China Sea

By CRISTIANO LIMA 05/03/2018 03:13 PM EDT

White House press secretary Sarah Huckabee Sanders said Thursday “there will be near-term and long-term consequences” in response to a reported build-up of defense systems in the South China Sea.

Sanders said the Trump administration has “raised concerns” about the measures “directly” with Chinese authorities.

The remarks came in response to reports that Chinese officials have installed anti-ship cruise missiles and surface-to-air missile systems on several of its outposts west of the Philippines in the South China Sea, a hotly-contested area in east Asia.

“We’re well aware of China’s militarization of the South China Sea,” Sanders said during the White House press briefing.

She added: “We’ve raised concerns directly with the Chinese about this, and there will be near-term and long-term consequences, and certainly we'll keep you up to date.”

https://www.cnbc.com/2018/05/03/white-house-china-faces-consequences-for-militarization-of-south-china-sea.html

White House warns China: There will be 'consequences' for militarization of South China Sea

The White House on Thursday warned China of "consequences" for its ongoing militarization of the hotly contested waters of the South China Sea.

Press secretary Sarah Huckabee Sanders issued the warning while she was responding to a question regarding CNBC's report that China had installed missile systems in outposts in the Spratly Islands.

"There will be near-term and long-term consequences," Sanders said.

Amanda Macias | Mike Calia

Published 58 Mins Ago Updated 13 Mins Ago


VCG/VCG | Getty Images
A PLA Navy fleet including the aircraft carrier Liaoning, submarines, vessels and fighter jets take part in a review in the South China Sea on April 12, 2018.

The White House on Thursday warned China of "consequences" for its ongoing militarization of the hotly contested waters of the South China Sea.

Press secretary Sarah Huckabee Sanders issued the warning while she was responding to a question regarding CNBC's report that China had installed missile systems in outposts in the Spratly Islands, which are two-thirds of the way east from southern Vietnam to the southern Philippines.

"We're well aware of China's militarization of the South China Sea," Sanders told reporters. "There will be near-term and long-term consequences."

The Pentagon also weighed in on the development.

"We have been very vocal about our concerns about them militarizing these artificial islands," Defense Department spokeswoman Dana White said. "China has to realize that they have benefited from the free navigation of the sea and the U.S. Navy has been the guarantor of that."

China quietly installed anti-ship cruise missiles and surface-to-air missile systems on three of its fortified outposts in the South China Sea, a move that allows Beijing to further project its power in the hotly disputed waters, sources with direct knowledge of U.S. intelligence reports had told CNBC.

Intelligence assessments say the missile platforms were deployed to Fiery Cross Reef, Subi Reef and Mischief Reef in the Spratly Islands within the past 30 days, according to sources who spoke on the condition of anonymity.

The placement of the defensive weapons also comes on the heels of China's recent South China Sea installation of military jamming equipment, which disrupts communications and radar systems.

The Spratlys, to which six countries lay claim, are located approximately two-thirds of the way east from southern Vietnam to the southern Philippines.


CSIS Asia Maritime Transparency Initiative/CNBC
Map of the South China Sea.

The new coastal defense systems represent a significant addition to China's military portfolio in one of the most contested regions in the world.

The South China Sea, which is home to more than 200 specks of land, serves as a gateway to global sea routes where approximately $3.4 trillion of trade passes annually.

The numerous overlapping sovereign claims to islands, reefs and rocks — many of which disappear under high tide — have turned the waters into an armed camp. Beijing holds the lion's share of these features with approximately 27 outposts peppered throughout.

The United States has remained neutral – but expressed concern – about the overlapping sovereignty claims to the Spratlys.

"The biggest dilemma is that Beijing could over time deploy many more missiles than Washington's naval missile defense platforms could defend against," Harry Kazianis, director of defense studies at the Center for the National Interest, told CNBC.

"At the end of the day, for Beijing, simple math means they would win a short-term military engagement and that spells all sorts of trouble for the U.S. Navy," he added.

The missile deployment is a sign that China will likely soon send warplanes to the area, too, according to an expert on the region.

"This should be seen as China crossing an important threshold. Missile platforms present a clear offensive threat," Gregory Poling, Center for Strategic and International Studies fellow and director of the Asia Maritime Transparency Initiative, told CNBC.

Amanda Macias
National security reporter

Mike Calia
Politics Editor

China installs cruise missiles on South China Sea outposts: CNBC

https://www.reuters.com/article/us-southchinasea-china-missiles/china-installs-cruise-missiles-on-south-china-sea-outposts-cnbc-report-idUSKBN1I336G?

WASHINGTON (Reuters) - China has installed anti-ship cruise missiles and surface-to-air missile systems on three of its outposts in the South China Sea, U.S. news network CNBC reported on Wednesday, citing sources with direct knowledge of U.S. intelligence reports.

The installations, if confirmed, would mark the first Chinese missile deployments in the Spratly Islands, where several Asian countries including Vietnam and Taiwan have rival claims.

China has made no mention of any missile deployments but says its military facilities in the Spratlys are purely defensive, and that it can do what it likes on its own territory.

CNBC quoted unnamed sources as saying that according to U.S. intelligence assessments, the missiles were moved to Fiery Cross Reef, Subi Reef and Mischief Reef within the past 30 days.
